dimensions.sota_bar: Virtual Token Counter (VTC) is the first work-conserving fair scheduler for LLM serving, tracking cumulative per-client input/output tokens to prevent any tenant monopolizing the batch; follow-ups (QLM, Prism, Equinox, FairBatching, PROSERVE) optimize multi-priority SLO attainment. At the gateway layer LiteLLM enforces per-key/project token budgets and spend caps. The SOTA bar is provable fairness + per-tenant SLO under contention, not raw throughput.
dimensions.why_it_matters: A shared cluster serving many agents/tenants must stop a noisy neighbor from starving everyone else and must hold each tenant's latency SLO under load. Fairness, quotas, and SLO-attainment-under-contention are exactly the properties a multi-tenant platform buyer evaluates.
dimensions.notes: VTC arXiv 2401.00588; gateway-side quota enforcement is the LiteLLM complement.

dimensions.dimension: Prompt-injection defense and agent security (attack-success-rate vs utility-under-attack)
dimensions.metric_unit: attack success rate %; utility-under-attack %; detector accuracy %
dimensions.sota_bar: AgentDojo (NeurIPS 2024; 97 tasks, 629 security cases) is the de facto agent-security benchmark: undefended GPT-4o keeps 69% benign utility but drops to 45% under attack with targeted ASR ~53% on the 'Important message' attack; strong defenses are needed to cut ASR while preserving utility. Detector guardrails: Llama Guard 3 ~99.7% on NotInject; Lakera Guard ~92.5% PINT / 98%+ vendor-stated detection at <50ms.
dimensions.why_it_matters: An agent that calls tools and reads untrusted content is an exfiltration surface; indirect prompt injection is the top agent-security risk. Buyers now require an ASR-vs-utility number, not a vibe, and adaptive attacks routinely break naive defenses, so the bar is benchmark-grounded.
dimensions.notes: ASR/utility numbers are model+attack specific; adaptive-attack papers (2503.00061) show defenses often regress.

dimensions.dimension: Tool/agent sandboxing, structural containment, and PII/exfil prevention
dimensions.metric_unit: qualitative (isolation guarantee); residual exfil rate
dimensions.sota_bar: Defense-in-depth beyond detectors: disposable isolated-container/VM sandboxes contain a compromised tool call, and structural/information-flow controls (capability allow-lists, tool-result parsing, provable defenses like MELON, taint/IFC on PII) bound what a hijacked agent can reach or exfiltrate. No single audited containment leaderboard exists; the SOTA bar is layered containment that holds even when the model is fooled.
dimensions.why_it_matters: Detectors are evadable, so the durable control is architectural: bound the blast radius so a successful injection still cannot read secrets, write outside scope, or exfiltrate PII. Structural containment is the property that survives an adaptive attacker and that security-conscious buyers actually demand.
dimensions.notes: Complements (does not replace) the detector/ASR dimension; few neutral numbers, so qualitative bar.

dimensions.dimension: Capability/quality of the served model (SWE-bench Verified, GPQA, AIME, LiveCodeBench)
dimensions.metric_unit: benchmark accuracy %
dimensions.sota_bar: Frontier models served by these engines define the quality bar buyers cite: SWE-bench Verified ~80.6% (Gemini 3.1 Pro) / 79.2% (Claude Opus 4.5 + Live-SWE-agent, Nov 2025); GPQA Diamond effectively saturated ~93-94% (Gemini 3 Deep Think 93.8%, GPT-5.4 92.8%); AIME 2025 up to 100% (GPT-5.4). These are model-served-by-engine results, not engine claims.
dimensions.why_it_matters: A serving stack only matters if it serves a model that can do the job; buyers pick the model on SWE-bench/GPQA/AIME first, then the engine to serve it. Engines must be framed as transport for these scores, and constrained decoding/scaffolding can move them, so the dimension belongs on the scorecard explicitly.
dimensions.notes: Numbers are model+scaffold specific and saturating fast; GPQA Diamond is near-saturated, weakening its discriminating power for 2026.

dimensions.dimension: Cross-agent fleet serving time (N agents × T turns): work eliminated by shared-prefix fusion
dimensions.why_it_matters: fak's product category. When a fleet of agents shares a large system/context prefix, fusing that prefix once across all agents eliminates re-prefill work that even a tuned per-agent cache repeats. This is the axis fak is built to win.
dimensions.sota_bar: A tuned warm per-agent KV cache (the SGLang / vLLM APC / OpenAI prompt-caching discipline) is the honest floor a serious operator already runs; fak measures the work eliminated ON TOP of it.
dimensions.metric_unit: min (fleet serving time) / × work eliminated

dimensions.dimension: Marginal value of cross-agent prefix fusion ON TOP of an already-warm per-agent cache
dimensions.why_it_matters: The honest few-fold: not the headline-vs-naive number, but the gain over the tuned warm-cache a real operator runs. This is the conservative claim a skeptic should be shown.
dimensions.sota_bar: An already-hot per-agent KV cache (1×). fak's cross-agent fusion adds a conservative 2.4–2.7× on top, measured on its own kernel held constant.
dimensions.metric_unit: × over a warm per-agent cache

dimensions.dimension: Cross-agent reuse marginal value vs a LIVE tuned shared-prefix engine (head-to-head)
dimensions.why_it_matters: A live SGLang/vLLM ALSO fuses a shared prefix once. The marginal win vs a real competing process — not vs fak's own warm-KV arm — is the number a buyer comparing engines actually wants, and fak has not measured it.
dimensions.sota_bar: SGLang/vLLM fuse a shared prefix once across requests; on the WebVoyager-style workload the marginal-vs-tuned win is only ~1.0–1.10×.
dimensions.metric_unit: × over a live tuned engine

dimensions.dimension: Single-stream (one chat) decode throughput on CPU
dimensions.why_it_matters: The regime local/edge stacks (llama.cpp, Ollama, MLC) compete hardest on: one user, one chat, raw tokens/s on commodity hardware. fak explicitly does NOT target this — showing the loss keeps the scorecard honest.
dimensions.sota_bar: llama.cpp Metal/CPU leads single-stream decode on the same box (e.g. Qwen2.5-7B Q8 ~17.3 tok/s Metal, M3 Pro).
dimensions.metric_unit: tok/s

dimensions.dimension: Single-stream prefill throughput, apples-to-apples CPU-vs-CPU
dimensions.why_it_matters: Prefill (prompt ingestion) speed on CPU is a core local-inference axis. fak's arm64 build lacks the register-blocked int8 GEMM tile that x86 already has — a disclosed, narrowing loss.
dimensions.sota_bar: llama.cpp CPU sets the prefill bar on the same box + Q8 weights (1× reference); fak arm64 is ~0.12× until the int8 tile lands.
dimensions.metric_unit: × of llama.cpp CPU

dimensions.dimension: Single-stream decode throughput on a consumer GPU
dimensions.why_it_matters: Local GPU single-stream decode (the RTX-class laptop/desktop regime) is where llama.cpp's CUDA backend sets the bar; fak reaches parity here at higher precision via a reusable CUDA graph.
dimensions.sota_bar: llama.cpp Q8_0 ~120 ± 15 tok/s on an RTX 4070 (-ngl 99).
dimensions.metric_unit: tok/s

dimensions.dimension: int8 / Q8_0 SIMD decode throughput vs the same-rung int8 peer
dimensions.why_it_matters: The execution speed of an int8-quantized model on CPU SIMD (AVX2/AVX-512) decides local-inference viability. fak's hand-written int8 GEMM beats the standard HuggingFace dynamic-int8 reference.
dimensions.sota_bar: HuggingFace dynamic-int8 is the standard same-rung CPU int8 reference (1×); llama.cpp Q8_0 CPU is the harder peer (~parity).
dimensions.metric_unit: × vs HF int8

dimensions.dimension: Dense GPU compute correctness with ZERO vendor GEMM (cuBLAS-free)
dimensions.why_it_matters: Every mainstream stack depends on NVIDIA cuBLAS for its GEMMs. Running a real model's dense path on an owned, dependency-free kernel — and proving it bit-matches the vendor oracle — is a portability/auditability differentiator.
dimensions.sota_bar: cuBLAS is the tuned vendor GEMM every other stack carries; matching it bit-for-bit (cosine=1.0) is parity by construction.
dimensions.metric_unit: cosine vs vendor-GEMM oracle
